metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises the full happy-path end-to-end against TestPyPI/staging: dispatch from main with correct versions → confirm tag is created → confirm downstream build+publish jobs run → confirm a TestPyPI upload succeeds (or a dry-run artifact is produced).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the full happy-path of the new primary release flow end-to-end (dispatch → version-check passes → tag auto-created → build/publish triggered → artifact published). AC3 verifies the flow only by reading the workflow YAML (structural check), not by running a real dispatch that produces a real tag and a real (staging/TestPyPI) publication. This is a cutover to a new code path with no criterion that exercises the new path as it will run in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; What section — two-phase flow
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '[ ] A workflow_dispatch run from `main` with matching versions completes end-to-end against TestPyPI/staging: the dispatch job creates the `v*` tag, the tag push triggers `build`/`publish`, and the publish job uploads a release artifact to TestPyPI (verified by the TestPyPI release URL in the job summary).' This satisfies E6(f) for the cutover path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the GitHub GITHUB_TOKEN loop-prevention gap: the dispatch job's tag-push step must use a credential that GitHub allows to trigger downstream push-event workflows (e.g., a scoped PAT stored as an Actions secret, or a GitHub App installation token), since GITHUB_TOKEN-created pushes do not trigger new workflow runs — document the chosen credential and update the Scope / AC3 to name it.
findings.finding: The two-phase relay mechanism — dispatch job pushes the v* tag using the default GITHUB_TOKEN, that tag push triggers the existing build/publish jobs — is broken by GitHub's loop-prevention policy: pushes made with GITHUB_TOKEN from within a workflow do NOT trigger new workflow runs (GitHub explicitly excludes `push`-triggered workflows from GITHUB_TOKEN-created events to prevent infinite loops). If the dispatch job uses GITHUB_TOKEN to push the tag, the `push: tags: v*` trigger will never fire and `build`/`publish`/`github_release`/`mcp_registry` will never run.
findings.location: What — Two-phase flow; AC3 ('that workflow-created tag push triggers build/publish')
findings.suggested_fix: In the dispatch job's tag-creation step, push the tag with a PAT (e.g. `secrets.RELEASE_PAT`) or a GitHub App installation token rather than the implicit GITHUB_TOKEN. Add a note to `docs/releasing.md` that this secret must be provisioned as a one-time setup step (similar to the existing PyPI pending-publisher setup). Update AC3 to specify the token mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope / workflow design that the new dispatch job requires a job-level `permissions: contents: write` override (not a workflow-level change) to push the v* tag, keeping all other jobs at the inherited `contents: read`.
findings.finding: The dispatch job must push a git tag (`git push origin vX.Y.Z`), which requires `contents: write`. The current `release.yml` declares `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow level (line 23–24), and no job-level override is specified in the plan for the new dispatch job. Without a job-level `permissions: contents: write` override, the tag-push step will fail with a 403.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml`; What — Two-phase flow; current release.yml line 23–24
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions: contents: write` as a job-level key on the new dispatch/version-check job in `release.yml`, mirroring the pattern already used on the `github_release` job (line 83).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the GITHUB_TOKEN re-trigger constraint: specify the credential/token the dispatch job uses to push the v* tag such that a `push: tags: v*` workflow event actually fires (GITHUB_TOKEN pushes do not trigger new runs per GitHub's loop-prevention policy), and document this in the plan before committing to the two-phase architecture.
findings.finding: The two-phase flow's core mechanism — the dispatch job pushes a v* tag using the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN, and that push fires a new push:tags workflow run — is an unverified capability assertion. GitHub Actions explicitly does NOT trigger new workflow runs from events created by the GITHUB_TOKEN (documented loop-prevention). The dispatch job pushing a tag with GITHUB_TOKEN would silently produce no downstream build/publish run, making the entire two-phase design inoperative without a PAT or deploy-key credential for the tag-push step.
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow' bullet; AC line 3: 'The dispatch job creates and pushes the v* tag only after the version-lockstep and ancestry checks pass; that workflow-created tag push is what triggers build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Options in order of minimal-new-secret overhead: (1) use `gh workflow run release.yml --ref vX.Y.Z` from the dispatch job instead of relying on push-event re-trigger (single workflow, no re-trigger needed); (2) push the tag via the existing Gerrit deploy key if it has tag-write access (it already can write refs); (3) use a GitHub App installation token scoped to `contents:write` (no long-lived PAT). Document the chosen approach in the 'Two-phase flow' bullet and in the relevant AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and justify the credential the dispatch job uses for its tag-push step — GitHub's loop-prevention policy means GITHUB_TOKEN will not trigger the downstream `push: tags: v*` workflow run; a PAT with `contents: write`, the Gerrit replication SSH deploy key (if it can be reused), or an explicit `gh workflow run` call are the viable options, and the chosen path must be stated in the plan with its auth precondition.
findings.finding: The two-phase relay's core mechanism — the dispatch job pushes a v* tag and that tag push triggers the existing build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry jobs via `push: tags: v*` — is broken by GitHub's loop-prevention policy. GitHub explicitly suppresses new workflow runs triggered by push events when the push is made using the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN`; the tag is created but no downstream workflow run fires. The plan never names the credential the dispatch job will use for the tag push, never acknowledges this GitHub platform constraint, and never states an auth precondition (PAT with `contents: write`, SSH deploy key, or alternative mechanism) for this specific new integration surface. The existing repo has `permissions: contents: read` at the release.yml workflow level, compounding the gap.
findings.location: What — Two-phase flow section; AC3 ('that workflow-created v* tag push triggers build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring at least one successful end-to-end dispatch run from `main` against a staging target (staging repo or TestPyPI) that demonstrates: version-check passes, ancestry assertion passes, `v*` tag is created and pushed by the workflow, and the downstream `build` job is triggered by that tag — so the happy path of the new default flow is behaviorally verified, not only structurally inspected.
findings.finding: The new default path — `workflow_dispatch` from `main` successfully completing version-check + ancestry assertion, then creating and pushing the `v*` tag, which triggers the downstream build/publish chain — is verified in AC3 only by 'the workflow definition' (a structural/definitional check), not by a live or staged end-to-end run. This is a proxy-validation gap on the cutover path: the suite can go green while the dispatch → tag-push → downstream-trigger chain is broken in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; 'Testing safely' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or augment AC3's 'verified by the workflow definition' clause with: 'AND verified by a successful dispatch run against the staging target where the workflow-created tag and the triggered downstream build job are both observable in the Actions run log (or equivalent staging evidence).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan which job (e.g. a new `pre-flight` job with `needs: [pre-flight]` added to `build`) carries the ancestry assertion on the fallback `v*` tag-push path, so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The plan requires the fallback raw `v*` tag-push path to run the 'same ancestry assertion' but does not specify which job or step in the workflow carries this out for the tag-push trigger path. An executor must decide independently whether to add a new pre-build job, insert a step into the existing `build` job, or use a separate gating job — a real design choice deferred to the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'Ancestry assertion' bullet; Scope section — `.github/workflows/release.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `release.yml` scope bullet: 'For the tag-push trigger, add a `pre-flight` job (with `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')`) that runs the ancestry assertion; `build` gains `needs: [pre-flight]` so a failed assertion aborts before any build step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete job/step name to the Scope's `release.yml` change list that handles the ancestry assertion on the raw v*-tag-push fallback path (e.g., a new `pre-build-guard` job triggered by `push` to `refs/tags/v*` that runs before `build`), so the What decomposition and Scope section agree on the implementation structure.
findings.finding: The What section's job decomposition does not specify which job or step enforces the ancestry assertion on the raw v*-tag-push fallback path, creating a gap between the described control ('the fallback path runs the same ancestry assertion') and the Scope section's change list, which describes the version-check job only in the dispatch-flow context.
findings.location: What section — 'Ancestry assertion' bullet; Scope section — 'release.yml'; AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for release.yml, add: 'a `pre-build-guard` job triggered by the v*-tag-push event that runs the ancestry assertion (fetch-depth: 0, git merge-base --is-ancestor) and fails the workflow before build if the tag is not a main ancestor — this is the fallback counterpart to the dispatch job's pre-tag assertion.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the `permissions:` block for the new `workflow_dispatch` dispatch job explicitly in the plan (minimally `contents: write` scoped to that job only, not elevated at workflow level), so the implementer does not over-grant or silently under-grant.
findings.finding: The plan's new `workflow_dispatch` dispatch job — which creates and pushes the `v*` tag to trigger the publication chain — is a new boundary-crossing component (tag push drives PyPI + MCP + GitHub Release publication) whose permission posture is never declared. The plan describes the job in detail (version-lockstep, ancestry assertion, tag creation) but never states what `permissions:` block it will carry. `contents: write` is required to push the tag; without an explicit declaration, the job will either silently inherit the workflow-level `contents: read` (tag push fails at runtime) or a future implementer adds `contents: write` at the wrong scope (e.g., workflow-level, re-granting write to all jobs).
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow' bullet; Scope section — `.github/workflows/release.yml`; AC line 3 ('The dispatch job creates and pushes the v* tag')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section and in the Scope bullet for release.yml, add: 'The dispatch job carries `permissions: contents: write` at job level (to push the v* tag); all other jobs retain their existing minimal permission declarations; the workflow-level `permissions: contents: read` default is unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC for release evidence (e.g. 'the dispatch job uploads a job summary that includes the SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes') or explicitly mark the evidence recording as optional/out-of-scope so the executor can make a clear decision.
findings.finding: The 'record release evidence' deliverable (approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, artifact hashes in a job summary / uploaded evidence file) appears in the What section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion. An executor cannot determine whether this is required in-scope work or an optional nice-to-have.
findings.location: What section — last bullet ('Record the approved SHA...'); Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that the dispatch/build jobs produce and upload release evidence (approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, artifact hashes) — e.g., as a job summary artifact — so the deliverable described in the What section is verifiable.
findings.finding: The 'release evidence' deliverable described in the What section has no corresponding acceptance criterion, leaving it unverifiable and potentially dropped during implementation.
findings.location: What section — final bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (a job summary / uploaded evidence file).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A release evidence file (or job summary) naming the approved SHA, tag, the four version sources, and build artifact hashes is produced and uploaded as a workflow artifact — verified by inspecting a completed dispatch-initiated release run's artifact list.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/probe AC that verifies the GitHub Actions environment deployment-policy evaluation behavior — specifically, confirm (via a test environment or GitHub docs citation) that a job triggered by a workflow-created tag push is evaluated against the tag ref (not the initiating dispatch ref) before committing to the two-phase design.
findings.finding: The two-phase dispatch→tag-push design rests on an unvalidated assumption about GitHub Actions platform behavior: that environment deployment-branch/tag policies are evaluated against the *triggering ref* (the v* tag created by the dispatch job) rather than the workflow's initiating ref (refs/heads/main). If this assumption is wrong, the environment gate either blocks the publish jobs or the two-phase design is unnecessary complexity. No spike or probe validates this GitHub platform behavior before the two-phase machinery is built.
findings.location: What section — Two-phase flow paragraph; AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A test environment (with a branch-restricted deployment environment) confirms that a job triggered by a workflow-created v* tag is approved by the environment policy, while a job in the same workflow run but not triggered by the tag is blocked — validating the two-phase design premise before building it.' Alternatively, cite the specific GitHub documentation section that specifies this evaluation order.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency behavior for the tag-creation step: document (and implement) a 'tag already exists and points to the same SHA → skip push and continue' guard, OR explicitly document the manual tag-deletion recovery procedure and add it to `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The tag-creation write in the dispatch job is not idempotent: if the job is retried after the tag was already pushed (e.g., due to a transient network failure after the push succeeds but before the job completes), re-running the dispatch will fail at `git push` (tag already exists), leaving the operator with an opaque error and no documented recovery path.
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow' / 'Dispatch job creates and pushes the v* tag'; Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the dispatch job's tag-creation step, before `git push`, check `git ls-remote --tags origin refs/tags/vX.Y.Z`; if the tag exists and its SHA matches `$GITHUB_SHA`, skip the push (idempotent success); if it exists but points to a different SHA, fail with a clear error message explaining the conflict and how to resolve it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a GitHub Actions `concurrency` group (e.g., `concurrency: group: release-${{ inputs.version }}, cancel-in-progress: false`) to the dispatch workflow to serialize simultaneous release attempts for the same version and prevent the TOCTOU race on tag creation.
findings.finding: Two simultaneous `workflow_dispatch` runs for the same version (e.g., two operators trigger dispatch concurrently) both pass the version-lockstep and ancestry checks independently, then race to push the same `v*` tag — a check-then-act TOCTOU on shared tag state. The second push fails with a git error, but the first may have already enqueued duplicate publish jobs or left the release in an inconsistent intermediate state.
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow'; 'Shared-state lifecycle' for the v* tag
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the `workflow_dispatch`-triggered workflow job (or workflow level):
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: release-${{ inputs.version }}
  cancel-in-progress: false
```
This serializes concurrent dispatches for the same version; `cancel-in-progress: false` ensures the second run waits rather than being silently dropped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in `docs/releasing.md` the full tag lifecycle including the RETIRE/cleanup procedure: when and how to delete an orphaned `v*` tag (e.g., after a failed or denied publish), how to re-trigger publication for an existing tag, and whether a tag-deletion + re-push re-triggers the pipeline.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new persistent shared-state artifact (the `v*` tag) but specifies no RETIRE/cleanup path: if the tag is created by the dispatch job but the subsequent `publish` job fails (e.g., PyPI rejects the upload, required-reviewer denies approval), the tag persists on the remote permanently. There is no documented procedure for deleting the tag to allow a corrected re-release, and the plan does not state whether re-pushing a tag at the same SHA would re-trigger the publish pipeline.
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow'; Acceptance Criteria AC3; Scope — `docs/releasing.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a non-happy-path AC (and doc section in `docs/releasing.md`) covering the post-tag-creation failure scenario: specify what an operator must do when the v* tag exists but publication was rejected, timed out, or failed (e.g., delete-and-recreate tag procedure, or a workflow-level tag-cleanup step on publish failure).
findings.finding: The plan covers failure-before-tag-creation paths thoroughly but does not address the partial-state scenario where the workflow-created v* tag has been pushed but the downstream publish job is subsequently abandoned or rejected at the required-reviewers environment gate — leaving a dangling tag that could block future release attempts or unexpectedly re-trigger the raw tag-push fallback.
findings.location: What section — Two-phase flow; Acceptance Criteria (AC#3, AC#7)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `docs/releasing.md` section and as a new AC, describe the recovery procedure: if the environment approval is rejected or publish fails after tag creation, the operator deletes the tag (`git push origin :refs/tags/vX.Y.Z`), fixes the root cause, and re-dispatches. Optionally add a workflow `on: workflow_run` or job `if: failure()` step that deletes the pushed tag when any downstream job fails, so state is automatically cleaned up.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88` citation in the 'Ancestry assertion' paragraph with an accurate reference — either cite `verify-identity.yaml:31` (which actually uses `fetch-depth: 0` for the same merge-base reason in a CI gate context) or remove the file:line citation and state the rationale directly.
findings.finding: The plan cites `reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88` as the location where 'the repo documents this' (that a shallow clone defeats ancestry), using it as codebase justification for the `fetch-depth: 0` + `git fetch origin main` design of the ancestry assertion. Reading lines 83-88 of reconcile-bridge.yml shows the comment there actually documents that 'A shallow graft defeats git's merge-base computation' in the context of the *tickets-store reconciler*'s SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT corruption bug — not as documentation of a release-gate ancestry-check pattern. The cited evidence is real but unrelated to the plan's claim.
findings.location: What section — Ancestry assertion paragraph: 'the repo documents this at `reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the repo documents this at reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88 and verify-identity.yaml already uses fetch-depth: 0' to 'verify-identity.yaml:31 already uses fetch-depth: 0 for this same reason in a CI gate context.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either bring the `pypi` GitHub environment (required-reviewers + deployment-branch restriction) under a `github_environment` / `github_repository_environment` Terraform resource in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` so its configuration is drift-detectable, OR explicitly document in the plan that out-of-band drift of this control is an accepted residual risk (with a named compensating control or monitoring step).
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub environment's required-reviewers configuration is named as a 'load-bearing control' by the plan but is managed entirely out-of-band from the IaC, leaving it vulnerable to silent drift (e.g., a GitHub admin removes reviewers or widens the deployment-branch restriction) with no Terraform-detectable guard.
findings.location: Scope section / AC: '[operator-attested] The live `pypi` GitHub environment has required reviewers enabled...' / What section: 'Human authorization — required-reviewers environment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `github_repository_environment` resource for `pypi` to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` specifying `reviewers` and `deployment_branch_policy { protected_branches = false; custom_branch_policies = true }` with a matching `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` restricting to `v*` tags. This makes `terraform plan` the drift detector for the required-reviewers gate — consistent with the plan's own principle of 'release-from-main + environment-approval are the load-bearing controls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit back-out/emergency-bypass procedure for the ancestry assertion on the fallback tag-push path (e.g., a named break-glass step such as temporarily disabling the pre-build-guard job or using a privileged workflow with audited approval), so the plan satisfies T4's requirement for an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism — not just acknowledgement of the behavioral change.
findings.finding: The ancestry assertion added to the raw v*-tag-push fallback path changes existing behavior — previously any v* tag triggered publication; now only tags on main-ancestor commits proceed — but the plan provides no rollback or back-out mechanism for emergency scenarios (e.g., a hotfix release from a non-main branch) where the assertion would block a legitimate release.
findings.location: What section — 'Ancestry assertion' bullet; AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Break-glass / emergency release' subsection to the What or Scope section: describe the documented procedure (e.g., a separate emergency workflow that requires two-human approval and records a justification artifact) for releasing from a non-main SHA, and note that this procedure is itself protected by the required-reviewers environment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the 'artifact hashes' sub-component of the release evidence file covers a gap not already served by the existing PEP 740 attestations (`attestations: true`, release.yml:73); if not, scope the evidence to SHA + tag + manifest versions only (the non-duplicative portion) and drop the artifact-hash recording.
findings.finding: The 'artifact hashes' component of the proposed release evidence file rebuilds functionality that PEP 740 attestations (already present at release.yml:73 with `attestations: true`) supply more robustly and with cryptographic binding. Recording artifact hashes in a separate uploaded evidence file adds complexity without a gap that existing attestations don't already cover.
findings.location: What section, final bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (a job summary / uploaded evidence file).'
findings.suggested_fix: Keep the GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY entry for approved SHA, tag, and manifest versions (lightweight, useful for audit). Remove 'artifact hashes' from the evidence scope — PEP 740 attestations already provide cryptographically-bound, verifiable artifact provenance. Reference `gh attestation verify` in the updated docs/releasing.md instead.

findings.finding: The `pypi` deployment environment is currently restricted to `v*` tag refs (`docs/releasing.md:25`: 'GitHub environment `pypi` exists, restricted to `v*` tags'). Under the new design, the `publish` job runs in a `workflow_dispatch`-triggered workflow context; GitHub evaluates deployment branch/tag policies against the triggering ref, not a tag the workflow itself creates mid-run. A `workflow_dispatch` ref is `refs/heads/main`, not a `v*` tag, so the `pypi` environment gate will block publication on every dispatch-from-main run — undermining the primary control.
findings.location: What section / 'Human authorization — required-reviewers environment' bullet; docs/releasing.md line 25; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and AC7, explicitly state that the `pypi` environment's deployment branch policy must be widened from 'restricted to v* tags only' to also permit deployment from `refs/heads/main` (or from `workflow_dispatch` triggered by main). Alternatively, restructure so that the tag-push fallback — now pointing at a workflow-created tag — is the sole deployment trigger, and the dispatch path ends after creating the tag and kicking off the tag-triggered run.

findings.finding: The plan's PRIMARY CONTROL is 'release initiated from the `main` ref via `workflow_dispatch`,' yet no AC verifies the workflow ENFORCES this constraint — i.e., that dispatching from a non-main ref is detected and rejected before any build/publish step. Without an enforcement check (e.g., a job-level `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` guard or an explicit fail step) and a corresponding AC that exercises the wrong-ref path, the dispatch-from-main guarantee is documentation-only, not machine-enforced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #1 / What section — 'Primary control — initiate from `main` via `workflow_dispatch`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Acceptance Criteria, add: '- [ ] A `workflow_dispatch` run targeting any ref other than `refs/heads/main` is rejected before any build/publish job starts — verified by dispatching from a feature branch and observing the workflow fail with a message naming the forbidden ref.' In `release.yml`, add a guard on the `version-check` job: `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` (or an explicit `run: if [ "$GITHUB_REF" != "refs/heads/main" ]; then echo "Must dispatch from main"; exit 1; fi` step).

findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub deployment environment is currently 'restricted to `v*` tags' (`docs/releasing.md:25`). GitHub evaluates environment deployment branch/tag policies against the *triggering* workflow ref, not a tag the workflow creates mid-run. A `workflow_dispatch` initiated from `refs/heads/main` does not satisfy a `v*` tag restriction, so the `publish` job's attempt to deploy to the `pypi` environment will be blocked by GitHub's own gate on every dispatch-initiated release — the primary control will silently fail at the environment gate.
findings.location: What section ('Human authorization — required-reviewers environment' bullet); AC7 ('operator-attested'); docs/releasing.md line 25
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and/or AC7, add an explicit step: update the `pypi` GitHub environment's deployment branch/tag policy to allow `refs/heads/main` (in addition to or instead of `refs/tags/v*`), so the dispatch-from-main path can reach the required-reviewers gate. Document this in `docs/releasing.md` under prerequisites.

findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata registers `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` with reason 'tag ruleset restricting v* writes', but the leaf plan body explicitly states 'infra/terraform-github/main.tf — no change'. This contradiction adds a deliverable (tag-push restriction) that the parent epic does not include in Finding 1's scope and actively treats as a resolved non-action — making the metadata diverge from both the leaf plan text and the parent contract.
findings.location: Leaf ticket fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 `file_impact` metadata entry for `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`
findings.suggested_fix: Update the leaf's file_impact entry for infra/terraform-github/main.tf: either delete it entirely (since the plan delivers no change there) or replace the reason with 'no change — deliberate no-tag-lock decision unchanged' to accurately reflect the plan's intent.

findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata entry for `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` carries the reason 'tag ruleset restricting v* writes', which directly contradicts the leaf's own plan text ('infra/terraform-github/main.tf — **no change**') and introduces a deliverable the parent's plan does not contain. The parent epic describes `main.tf:23-28` tags as 'deliberately left unprotected' and Finding 1 is scoped to the ancestry/version gate — not a tag-push ruleset. An implementer following the `file_impact` metadata would write a Terraform tag ruleset that is out of scope for both the leaf and the parent.
findings.location: Leaf ticket `file_impact` metadata / `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` entry
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` entry from `file_impact` entirely (it is a no-change file), or replace the reason with 'no change — tag-lock deliberately out of scope per plan' to make the metadata self-consistent and aligned with the parent.

findings.finding: The plan makes `workflow_dispatch` the primary publication trigger, but the three downstream jobs `publish`, `github_release`, and `mcp_registry` all gate on `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` (lines 53, 81, 116). The Scope section does not enumerate updating these `if:` conditions as required edits, so the plan's primary control path would silently skip all publication steps if implemented as-described.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release.yml` lines 53, 81, 116

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the two-phase dispatch→tag-push architectural decision and its rationale (Gerrit-mirror topology, pypi environment v*-tag policy, why single-phase dispatch won't satisfy the existing environment restriction) in an ADR or a dedicated section in CLAUDE.md, not only in the operational releasing.md.
findings.finding: The novel two-phase dispatch→tag pipeline design — and the architectural rationale for why it is needed given this project's Gerrit-mirror topology and pypi environment policy — is documented only in docs/releasing.md (operational runbook) and inline in the plan, but not captured in any ADR or CLAUDE.md entry. A future maintainer who considers simplifying the release flow (e.g., removing the two-phase indirection) would have no durable record of why the design was chosen.
findings.location: Scope section; What section — Two-phase flow paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., docs/adr/release-two-phase-dispatch.md) or a CLAUDE.md section titled 'Release flow architecture' that records: (1) the constraint (pypi environment evaluates against triggering ref), (2) the decision (two-phase: dispatch creates tag, tag triggers publish), (3) consequences (cannot simplify to single-phase without changing the environment policy).
